frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: encoding and decoding optimally adapted to sensory input statistics

frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: decoding stage interprets encoded signal given input prior

frameworks.name: Wei & Stocker observer model
frameworks.one_line: extends 2015 perception theory to a closed-form bias-threshold link

core_question: Are perceptual bias and discrimination threshold independent measures, or are they tightly coupled by a single underlying law?
key_assumptions: sensory encoding and decoding are jointly optimized to input statistics
the same input prior constrains both bias and threshold
the bias-threshold link is invariant across sensory variables
manipulations: sensory variable type (orientation, spatial frequency, motion direction, pursuit, heading, biological motion)
key_findings: perceptual bias and discrimination threshold are not independent but linked
a simple mathematical relationship (up to scale and offset) predicts one from the other
the relationship holds across many published data-sets and sensory modalities
the relationship also holds under context-induced changes (adaptation, learning, surround)
interpretations: bias and threshold reflect a shared origin in input-statistics-adapted encoding/decoding
the derived link constitutes a new psychophysical law supporting a holistic theory of perception
